target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Existing jobs using credential URLs will break. - Mitigation: Document in release notes; credential URLs are discouraged; alternative auth methods should be used.

target.title: fix(cron): scope all-run job names
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #49175.

`cron.runs` with `scope: "all"` still returns global run-log rows, but job-name enrichment now uses the same agent-scoped job visibility path as `cron.list` instead of reading every job through the unfiltered `cron.list()` shortcut.

## Problem summary

`cron.runs(scope=all)` built `jobNameById` from `context.cron.list({ includeDisabled: true })`. That list is not caller/agent filtered, so run-history responses could attach another agent's cron job name to otherwise global run entries.

## Root cause

The all-scope run-log reader intentionally scans all run logs, but the server method reused an unscoped job list to enrich those entries and to make job names searchable. The leak was in the enrichment map, not in scheduler execution or run-log storage.

## Architectural reasoning

- Reuses `context.cron.listPage()` for the job-name lookup because it already applies `agentId` filtering and effective default-agent handling.
- Paginates through visible jobs with `limit: 200` so the enrichment map remains complete for large visible job lists.
- Accepts optional `agentId` on `CronRunsParams`; when omitted, enrichment defaults to the cron service default agent, then the canonical `main` default.
- Leaves run-log scanning, job-specific `cron.runs`, scheduling, delivery, cleanup, retry, and background-task behavior unchanged.
- Keeps backward compatibility for the run-history shape: global entries still return, but entries outside the visible agent scope are not enriched with private job names.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/cron.runs.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/cron-validators.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/gateway/protocol/schema/cron.ts src/gateway/protocol/cron-validators.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts src/gateway/server-methods/cron.runs.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `cron.runs(scope=all)` no longer builds its job-name enrichment map from every cron job across all agents.
- Real environment tested: Local Windows source checkout, Node `v22.15.0`, pnpm `10.33.2`. The repo warns that Node `>=22.16.0` is preferred on this machine; the targeted proof below completed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the patched `cron.runs` server handler from the source checkout with `scope: "all"` and `agentId: "main"`; the command throws if the old unscoped `cron.list()` path is used and records the actual listing call made by the handler.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the patched checkout:

```json
{
  "unscopedListCalls": 0,
  "listPageCalls": [
    {
      "includeDisabled": true,
      "agentId": "main",
      "limit": 200,
      "offset": 0
    }
  ],
  "response": {
    "ok": true,
    "result": {
      "entries": [],
      "total": 0,
      "offset": 0,
      "limit": 1,
      "hasMore": false,
      "nextOffset": null
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The patched handler did not call the old unscoped list path (`unscopedListCalls: 0`) and used the agent-scoped paginated list path with `agentId: "main"`; the call returned successfully.
- What was not tested: A full live gateway/UI cron-history session was not run on this Windows host. The existing `src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts -t "writes cron run history"` integration test was attempted locally and still fails here because the pre-existing all-scope run-log directory listing path returns no entries under the local fs-safe/root implementation. That failure is independent of this privacy fix; the added regression directly tests the changed handler contract.

## Risk analysis

Risk is low-to-medium. The changed runtime path is limited to all-scope `cron.runs` job-name enrichment. It does not alter run execution, scheduler concurrency, cleanup, delivery, retries, or job-specific run-history reads. The main compatibility change is intentionally privacy-preserving: callers still receive all-scope run entries, but job names are only attached for the requested/default agent scope.

## Backward compatibility

Additive protocol change: `CronRunsParams` now accepts optional `agentId`. Existing `cron.runs` callers remain valid.

target.title: fix(approvals): summarize long approval prompts
target.body:
## Problem summary

Fixes #58687. Long exec approval prompts can render enough command text that the approval controls become hard to reach or review, especially for inline scripts. The Control UI also rendered an Allow Always button even when the gateway omitted `allow-always` from the request's allowed decisions, making that action look ineffective.

## Root cause

Exec approval request payloads were already sanitized/redacted before display, but presentation surfaces still rendered the whole sanitized command up to the existing display cap. The Control UI parser also ignored `request.allowedDecisions`, so it could offer a decision that the gateway would reject for `ask: "always"` or other restricted approval modes.

## Architectural reasoning

- Adds a browser-safe command prompt summarizer in `src/infra` and uses it only at presentation boundaries: fallback forwarded approval text, shared approval reply payloads, native approval view models, and the Control UI modal.
- Leaves the pending approval record, sanitized command payload, `systemRunPlan`, bindings, and resolution path unchanged, so command identity and allow-always matching are not loosened.
- Preserves short command rendering exactly and only summarizes when the prompt exceeds five logical lines or 1,200 display chars.
- Teaches the Control UI approval parser to carry `allowedDecisions` for exec and plugin approval requests, then renders only those actions in the modal.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: long exec approval command text is summarized before prompt rendering, and Control UI approval parsing preserves the gateway-provided allowed decisions so unavailable Allow Always choices can be omitted.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `hemant/58687-approval-prompt-summary` at `aaa32699bfff52d4147d2850f456eaa2c88f3dbe`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Imported the actual runtime helper from `src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts` with `pnpm exec tsx`.
  2. Built an eight-line approval command using the same escaped line-break marker shape that sanitized approval command text can contain.
  3. Parsed a synthetic gateway `exec.approval.requested` payload through the actual Control UI parser with `allowedDecisions: ["allow-once", "deny"]`.
  4. Printed the resulting prompt summary and parsed decisions.
- Evidence after fix:
```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { summarizeExecApprovalCommandForPrompt } from './src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts'; import { parseExecApprovalRequested } from './ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.ts'; const command = Array.from({ length: 8 }, (_, i) => 'echo line ' + (i + 1) + '\\u{A}').join(''); const summary = summarizeExecApprovalCommandForPrompt(command); const parsed = parseExecApprovalRequested({ id: 'proof-1', request: { command: 'echo ok', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'] }, createdAtMs: 1, expiresAtMs: 2 }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ summaryTruncated: summary.truncated, hiddenLineCount: summary.hiddenLineCount, includesLine8: summary.text.includes('line 8'), summaryPreview: summary.text.split('\n').slice(0, 6), parsedAllowedDecisions: parsed?.request.allowedDecisions }, null, 2));"
{
  "summaryTruncated": true,
  "hiddenLineCount": 4,
  "includesLine8": false,
  "summaryPreview": [
    "echo line 1\\u{A}",
    "echo line 2\\u{A}",
    "echo line 3\\u{A}",
    "echo line 4\\u{A}",
    "echo line 5\\u{A}",
    "...[truncated: showing first 5 of 9 lines; 52 chars hidden]"
  ],
  "parsedAllowedDecisions": [
    "allow-once",
    "deny"
  ]
}
```
- Observed result after fix: the prompt summary is truncated, hides the tail (`includesLine8: false`), reports the hidden content, and the parser returns only `allow-once` and `deny`, which is the decision set the modal will render.
- What was not tested: I did not attach a screenshot because the browser modal path is covered by `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.browser.test.ts`; I did not run a live gateway approval through an external chat account because the changed code is presentation-only and the native adapter tests cover Matrix, Slack, and Telegram rendering.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.browser.test.ts src/infra/approval-handler-runtime.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm lint:docs`
- `pnpm tsgo:core` via temporary `P:\` mapping because direct execution from `C:\Projects\Personal Project\openclaw` still trips the path-with-spaces launcher issue
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` via temporary `P:\` mapping
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` via temporary `P:\` mapping
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

`pnpm test:changed` fanned out to the broad `unit-fast` shard and failed on unrelated Windows/local-environment assumptions: POSIX path expectation mismatches, table glyph fallback expectations, SQLite temp cleanup `EBUSY`, missing A2UI scaffold returning 404, and Testbox local-key fixture assumptions. The focused approval, UI, and native adapter tests above passed.

## Risk analysis

Low-to-medium. The changed behavior is intentionally display-only except for honoring already-present `allowedDecisions` in the Control UI. Approval matching, pending record storage, node/gateway resolution, command binding, and execution semantics are unchanged. The main risk is that native approval clients now receive summarized `view.commandText` for long commands, covered by native handler tests across Matrix, Slack, and Telegram.

## Screenshots/logs

No screenshot was needed; the browser approval modal regression test covers the rendered modal path. The real behavior proof and test command summaries above are the relevant logs.

## Backward compatibility

Existing approval request payload shape is compatible. Clients that do not send `allowedDecisions` still see the historical default actions. Short command prompts render unchanged; long prompts are summarized only in human-facing approval surfaces while the full sanitized command remains bound to the approval record.

target.title: fix(acp): honor tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk for thought-stream events
target.body:
## Summary

Honor the operator-tunable `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk` flag for ACP thought-stream `text_delta` events. Closes catalog finding #12.

## Bug detail

Wire probe captured 2 `agent_thought_chunk` sessionUpdates per turn from copilot's ACP runtime. The deployed openclaw config set `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk: true`, but the operator never saw the thoughts in any channel. The projector at `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:408-411` had a hard `event.stream !== "output"` guard that ran BEFORE the `isAcpTagVisible` check, so the operator-tunable visibility flag was structurally unreachable for thought-stream events.

## How to reproduce

Unit-level repro in this branch before the fix commit (`f0180a023c`):

```bash
git checkout f0180a023c
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts
# Expected: 'delivers thought chunks when tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk=true' FAILS
```

After the fix commit (`379320c173`) the same test passes.

## Root cause

`src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts:408-411` (pre-fix):

```ts
if (event.type === \"text_delta\") {
  if (event.stream && event.stream !== \"output\") {
    return; // drops ALL non-\"output\" streams unconditionally, including \"thought\"
  }
  if (!isAcpTagVisible(settings, event.tag)) {
    return;
  }
}
```

The `acpx` translator faithfully tags the event with `stream: \"thought\"`; the projector unconditionally discarded it before consulting visibility settings.

## Fix approach

Reorder the guards: `isAcpTagVisible` runs FIRST and is authoritative; only if visibility is denied does the function return. The stream check is then narrowed so that \"output\" and \"thought\" streams both fall through to delivery, while other unknown streams (e.g., a hypothetical `stream: \"diagnostic\"`) still drop. Default behavior unchanged: `tagVisibility.agent_thought_chunk: false` (default) keeps thought events hidden.

## Tests

- **Red test (now GREEN):** `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts` — 3 scenarios cover default-hidden, visibility-true (the fix), and output-stream control.
- **Adjacent suite:** `src/auto-reply/reply/` — 1538 tests pass / 0 fail (115 files).

## Catalog reference

Catalog finding: #12. Investigation lives in branch `edpiva/acp-native-session-investigation` (not yet upstream) at `docs/plans/2026-05-08-acp-findings-catalog.md`.

## Verification commands

\`\`\`bash
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.thought-chunk-delivery.test.ts   # GREEN
pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/                                              # 1538 pass / 0 fail
\`\`\`

## Notes for reviewers

The new guard correctly drops unknown-stream events (e.g., `stream: \"diagnostic\"`) regardless of `tagVisibility`, but no test exercises that path explicitly. The behavior is the desired one — coverage gap only, not a wrong fix. Happy to add a fourth test case if you want it.

---

## Live behavior repro (deployed container)

**Container:** `codeclaw-openclaw:clean` running OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (`e9d4a0a`)
**PII note:** UUIDs are random; nothing to redact in this slice.

### Operator opted into thought delivery, copilot emitted thought events, projector dropped them all

Live config in the container:

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw jq '.acp.stream.tagVisibility' /home/codeclaw/.openclaw/openclaw.json
{
  "tool_call": true,
  "tool_call_update": true,
  "agent_thought_chunk": true,
  "plan": true
}
```

Operator has `agent_thought_chunk: true` set (i.e., wants thoughts delivered).

Copilot's `events.jsonl` for the relevant ACP session shows the events arrived on the wire:

```
$ docker exec codeclaw-openclaw grep -c agent_thought_chunk \
    /home/codeclaw/.copilot/session-state/882435ee-…/events.jsonl
2
```

Pre-fix, those events were silently dropped at `acp-projector.ts:408-411` because the unconditional `event.stream !== "output"` guard ran BEFORE `isAcpTagVisible` could consult the operator-tunable visibility flag. Operator never saw a thought chunk, even after explicitly opting in.

After this PR (fix `379320c173` + coverage `bdf2df35ab`), `isAcpTagVisible` runs first and is authoritative, then the stream allow-list narrows to `"output" | "thought"`. Hypothetical unknown streams (e.g., `"diagnostic"`) still drop regardless of `tagVisibility` — covered by the 4th test added in the cleanup commit.

commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=df02c6738e0a37aba744add1b595e45f682563dc)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a generated Microsoft ecosystem tracker file, a GitHub CLI script to regenerate it or replace a PR body, and an unreleased changelog entry.

PR surface: Docs +420, Other +428. Total +848 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review blockers: source inspection shows the raw PR-body update path, and live GitHub metadata reports three closing issue references caused by the generated body. I did not run the generator because this review is read-only and no latest-head contributor proof was provided.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human handling is needed because the PR is protected/draft, lacks contributor proof, and needs a maintainer decision on repo automation versus PR-body process tracking.

target.title: WORKING: All Microsoft Issues and PRs (refresh)
target.body:
# Microsoft Ecosystem Issues & PRs Tracker

> **Purpose:** Living checklist for maintainers to track all open Microsoft-related issues and PRs (Teams, Windows, WSL, Azure, M365/SharePoint).
>
> **How to use:**
>
> - Mark items resolved by editing this PR body and changing `[ ]` to `[x]`
> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale
> - Items marked _(stale)_ have been flagged by the stale bot due to inactivity
>
> **Last updated:** 2026-06-23 (post-purge audit: refreshed from currently open GitHub issues/PRs and rebuilt from PR #49126 format)

---

## Summary

| Category | Issues | PRs | Total | Closed | Remaining |
| -------- | ------ | --- | ----- | ------ | --------- |
| MS Teams (channel plugin) | 11 | 44 | 55 | 0 | 55 |
| Windows platform | 52 | 39 | 91 | 0 | 91 |
| WSL | 9 | 1 | 10 | 0 | 10 |
| Azure | 8 | 3 | 11 | 0 | 11 |
| SharePoint / M365 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **80** | **87** | **167** | **0** | **167** |

---

| Windows platform | pr | P0 | #85264 | fix(infra): scope Windows path realpath caches |
| Windows platform | pr | P0 | #81443 | fix: resolve QMD Windows shims and guard image URL downloads |

## Audit Notes

- Rebuilt from the format of PR #49126 after the issue/PR purge.
- Source set is currently open GitHub issues and PRs from `openclaw/openclaw`; closed counts are intentionally reset to `0` for this refreshed tracker.

- Generated with `node scripts/generate-microsoft-tracker.mjs` so the tracker and PR body can be refreshed after future triage passes.

target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the loop-aware compaction guard plan needed a small, reviewable config/schema landing point before detector/runtime work.
- Why it matters: separating config scaffolding from runtime behavior keeps review narrow and lets follow-up PRs build on a stable config surface.
- What changed: added `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` types, schema validation, conservative default materialization, docs/help/labels, and focused regression tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no detector, no scoring, no runtime hook, no automatic reset behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #48238

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Config docs/schema now expose a reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block.
- `loadConfig()` materializes `agents.defaults.compaction.guard.enabled = false` when compaction defaults are materialized.
- No runtime compaction behavior changes.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.3.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: local host checkout
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): targeted config fixtures under `src/config/*`

### Steps

1. Add `compaction.guard` fields to agent-default types and zod schema.
2. Materialize conservative defaults in `applyCompactionDefaults()` without adding runtime guard logic.
3. Update config docs/help/labels and add focused regression/default tests.
4. Run targeted config test wrapper.

### Expected

- Reserved guard config parses and documents correctly.
- Invalid enum/range values are rejected.
- Defaulted guard shape is present without changing runtime behavior.

### Actual

- Targeted config tests passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: explicit valid `compaction.guard` config, defaulted disabled guard shape, invalid escalation rejection, invalid numeric bounds rejection, docs/help parity.
- Edge cases checked: guard block remains config-only and does not add runtime compaction behavior.
- What you did **not** verify: detector/scorer/runtime follow-up behavior (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commit `206c7b018f045f967b2578e2679f8674a9eb7581` or remove the reserved `agents.defaults.compaction.guard` block from config.
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/*` compaction schema/default files and `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected config parsing/defaulting regressions under `agents.defaults.compaction`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: default materialization under `agents.defaults.compaction` could subtly affect config snapshots/load expectations.
  - Mitigation: focused regression/default tests cover explicit config, defaulted shape, and rejection cases.

commands.author: zhangqueping

target.title: fix(ui): reconcile model dropdown cache with server-resolved model after sessions.patch
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: The WebChat model dropdown shows the user-selected model (e.g. `codex/gpt-5.5`) while the server silently falls back to the default model (e.g. `ollama/qwen3.5:9b`), causing a misleading mismatch between the displayed and effective runtime model. The `/model` slash command already reconciles the dropdown with the server-resolved model from `sessions.patch`, but the dropdown selection path caches the requested value immediately and never updates it with the actual server-resolved model.

**Solution**: After the dropdown triggers `sessions.patch`, read `patched.resolved.model` and `patched.resolved.modelProvider` from the response and update the `chatModelOverrides` cache so the picker shows the effective runtime model. This reconciliation only applies when the user selects an explicit model (non-empty string). When the user selects Default (empty string), the cache stays as `null` so the selector keeps following the current default model — preventing a Default-clearing regression where picking Default would be incorrectly cached as an explicit resolved model override.

**What changed**: One file — `ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts`. In `switchChatModel`, after awaiting `sessions.patch`, the code now reads `patched.resolved` and reconciles the `chatModelOverrides` cache with the server-resolved model, but only when `nextModel` is non-empty. The `nextModel` dependency (`SessionsPatchResult`) was added to the import from `../types.ts::SessionsPatchResult`. The helper `resolvePreferredServerChatModelValue` and `createChatModelOverride` were added to the import from `../chat-model-ref.ts`.

**What did NOT change**:
- No changes to the server-side model routing or fallback logic — this is strictly a UI display fix
- No changes to the `/model` slash command path — that code already reconciles correctly
- No changes to `chat-model-ref.ts`, `chat-model-select-state.ts`, or any other UI module
- No config, migration, auth, or protocol changes
- The proof-policy parser regex in `scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-policy.mjs` was intentionally left untouched — this PR focuses only on the WebChat dropdown fix

Fixes #93346

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: The chat model dropdown shows the user-selected model while the server silently falls back to a different provider/model, causing the displayed model to differ from the effective runtime model. The `/model` slash command already reconciles correctly using `patched.resolved`, but the dropdown selection path did not.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 22.19, Linux x86_64
- **Repository**: openclaw/openclaw at commit `0f5cdafd8ea5`
- **Code analysis path**: Full trace through `switchChatModel` → `sessions.patch` → server response → cache reconciliation
- **Test framework**: Vitest 4.1.8

**Root cause**: `switchChatModel` in `ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts` immediately writes `createChatModelOverride(nextModel)` into `chatModelOverrides` before the RPC round-trip completes. This pre-caching is intentional — it makes the picker feel instant rather than waiting for the network. After `sessions.patch` returns, the response's `patched.resolved` field — which contains the server's actual resolved model after applying auth checks, provider availability, and fallback rules — is never read or used to update the cache. The `/model` slash command in `slash-command-executor.ts:218` already reads `patched.resolved` and builds a `modelOverride` from it, proving the reconciliation contract exists and works. The dropdown path simply never consumed the same data.

The data flow for the broken path is:
1. User picks `codex/gpt-5.5` → dropdown calls `switchChatModel(state, "codex/gpt-5.5")`
2. Before RPC: `chatModelOverrides[sessionKey] = { kind: "qualified", value: "codex/gpt-5.5" }`
3. RPC fires `sessions.patch` with `model: "codex/gpt-5.5"`
4. Server tries Codex provider → auth fails or provider unreachable → falls back to default `ollama/qwen3.5:9b`
5. Server returns `{ resolved: { model: "qwen3.5:9b", modelProvider: "ollama" } }`
6. **Bug**: The response is discarded — the UI keeps showing `codex/gpt-5.5`
7. User thinks they are using GPT-5.5 but the session is actually running on the local model

The fix inserts step 6a: read `patched.resolved`, call `resolvePreferredServerChatModelValue` + `createChatModelOverride`, and update the cache if the resolved value differs from the current entry. This matches the `/model` command path exactly.

Additionally, the original patch unconditionally applied reconciliation even when the user selected Default (empty string). When `nextModel` is empty, `createChatModelOverride("")` returns `null`, which is the correct Default sentinel in the cache — it tells `resolveChatModelSelectState` (in `chat-model-select-state.ts:38`) to return an empty override, which the selector renders as "Default" and keeps following the current system default model. But the reconciliation code would then read `patched.resolved.model` — which the server fills with the current default model — and write it back as an explicit override like `{ kind: "qualified", value: "ollama/qwen3.5:9b" }`, permanently breaking Default semantics. The selector would never again show "Default" for that session, and if the system default model changed later, this session would be stuck on the old default. The fix guards reconciliation behind `if (nextModel)` so Default selections remain as `null` in the cache.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
cd ui && npx vitest run \
  src/ui/chat-model-select-state.test.ts \
  src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts \
  --config vitest.config.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw && \
  python3 ../.claude/skills/pr-killer/scripts/check_pr.py
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
✓ src/ui/chat-model-select-state.test.ts (9 tests | 9 passed)
✓ src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (23 tests | 23 passed)
Test Files  2 passed (2)
     Tests  32 passed (32)
```

```
🔍 PR 提交前合规检查...
  ✅ fork 已就绪: zhangqueping/openclaw
  ✅ 无 lockfile 变更
  ℹ️  当前分支: fix/issue-93346
✅ 合规检查通过
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `chatModelOverrides` cache is updated from `patched.resolved` only when the user explicitly selects a non-empty model from the dropdown. The complete behavior matrix after the fix:

| User action | `nextModel` | Cache entry | Display |
|---|---|---|---|
| Select `codex/gpt-5.5`, server resolves to same | `"codex/gpt-5.5"` | `{ kind: "qualified", value: "codex/gpt-5.5" }` | `gpt-5.5 · codex` |
| Select `codex/gpt-5.5`, server falls back to `ollama/qwen3.5:9b` | `"codex/gpt-5.5"` | `{ kind: "qualified", value: "ollama/qwen3.5:9b" }` | `qwen3.5:9b · ollama` |
| Select Default from dropdown | `""` (empty) | `null` (unchanged) | "Default" — follows current default |
| Select Default, server default changes later | `""` (empty) | `null` (unchanged) | "Default" — automatically tracks new default |

The `JSON.stringify` comparison ensures we only trigger a state update when the resolved value genuinely differs from the current cache entry, avoiding unnecessary UI re-renders when the server confirms the requested model without changes. On RPC failure, the `catch` block restores `prevOverride` so the picker reflects the last known good server state instead of being stuck.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end testing with an actual gateway and server-side model fallback scenario (e.g., selecting a Codex model when the Codex API key is invalid and the server falls back to a local Ollama model). This requires a running gateway instance with at least two providers configured — one reachable and one unreachable — plus a browser to interact with the WebChat dropdown. The fix is validated through source-level analysis of the full reconciliation path (matching the shipped `/model` command behavior) and existing test coverage that exercises `createChatModelOverride`, `resolvePreferredServerChatModelValue`, and `resolveChatModelOverrideValue`. The Default regression fix is validated by code review of the `if (nextModel)` guard and the pre-existing behavior of `createChatModelOverride("")` returning `null`.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 32 related tests pass (9 chat-model-select-state + 23 chat-model-ref) |
| Compliance | ✅ | check_pr.py passes — fork ready, no lockfile changes |

## Design decisions

**Why guard with `if (nextModel)` instead of `if (nextModel !== "")`?**

JavaScript's truthiness check handles both `""` (Default selection) and any unexpected falsy values (`null`, `undefined`) consistently. The dropdown value is always a string — empty for Default, or a qualified model reference like `codex/gpt-5.5`. Using truthiness rather than strict equality is a defensive choice that also handles edge cases where a future caller might pass an unexpected falsy value.

**Why use `JSON.stringify` instead of deep equality?**

The `ChatModelOverride` type is a simple discriminated union with two variants, each containing two keys. `JSON.stringify` on these small objects is sufficiently fast and avoids importing a deep-equality utility for a single comparison site. The comparison prevents unnecessary state updates when the server confirms the requested model without any fallback — a common case where reconciliation would otherwise trigger a spurious re-render.

**Why revert the proof-policy parser regex change?**

The original branch included a commit that modified `scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-policy.mjs` to swap the order of `(?:\\*\\*)?` and `(?:\\s*\\([^)]*\\))?` in `fieldLineRegex`. That change addressed a CI proof-gate parsing issue unrelated to the WebChat dropdown bug. Mixing a proof-gate automation change into a UI bugfix PR creates unnecessary review surface and could independently affect ClawSweeper's proof-gate behavior for all external PRs. The parser fix should be proposed in its own PR with dedicated test coverage for the affected regex patterns.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**
The `if (nextModel)` guard is the highest-risk area. It determines whether reconciliation runs or is skipped. If `nextModel` is falsy when the user actually selected a real model (which shouldn't happen because `nextModel` is the dropdown value — empty only for Default), reconciliation would be incorrectly skipped. Conversely, if the guard were absent, selecting Default would cache the resolved default model as an explicit override, permanently breaking Default-following behavior. The guard is intentionally simple and uses JavaScript's truthiness check — empty string is the only falsy value that reaches this code path for Default selection.

**Risk level**: Low risk — the change is narrowly scoped to a single function in a single file, guarded by a simple truthiness check, and the reconciliation logic closely follows the existing pattern in `slash-command-executor.ts:218` which has been shipped and working.

**Mitigation**: The existing 32 unit tests covering `chat-model-ref.ts` and `chat-model-select-state.ts` exercise the `createChatModelOverride`, `resolvePreferredServerChatModelValue`, and selector resolution paths that this change depends on. The `JSON.stringify` comparison prevents unnecessary state updates. The `catch` block rolls back the cache to the previous override on RPC failure, preserving the existing error-recovery contract.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 session-state` — The change modifies how `chatModelOverrides` cache is populated, which affects per-session UI model state. However, the change is gated on non-empty `nextModel` and only runs after a successful RPC, minimizing the risk window.
- `merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility` — Users who previously relied on the dropdown showing their requested model (even when the server silently fell back) will now see the effective model. This is the intended behavior correction and matches the `/model` command.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. The proof parser regex change that was originally in this branch has been reverted — it belongs in a separate PR.

commands.author: SunnyShu0925

target.title: fix(memory-core): harden dreaming daily-file writes and drain dangling recall refs
target.body:
## Summary

Memory-core dreaming daily-file writes could truncate files on crash because `fs.writeFile` is non-atomic. Additionally, the recall store accumulates dangling references when daily memory files are silently deleted, wasting promotion cycles on dead entries. This PR hardens the write path with atomic rename and drains dangling recall references during repair.

- **Problem**: (1) `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock` and `writeDeepDreamingReport` use `fs.writeFile` (non-atomic on Linux), so a process crash mid-write can truncate daily memory or dreaming report files. (2) When daily memory files disappear (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths are never cleaned up — the promotion pipeline wastes cycles attempting to rehydrate them and silently fails. (3) ClawSweeper P1: manual `tmp+randomUUID+rename` loses file permissions — a new file gets the default umask mode instead of preserving the existing file's mode. (4) ClawSweeper P1: dangling-ref pruning catch block silently swallows `EACCES`/`EPERM`, treating permission-denied files as missing and incorrectly deleting valid recall entries.
- **Solution**: (1) Use `replaceFileAtomic` from `@openclaw/fs-safe` for all three dreaming write paths — it atomically writes via temp+rename and supports `preserveExistingMode`. (2) In `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`, detect entries whose source file no longer exists on disk and remove them, surfacing the count in the log summary. (3) Replace manual `tmp-${randomUUID()}+writeFile+rename` with `replaceFileAtomic({ preserveExistingMode: true })` for inline daily writes, preserving the existing file's permission bits. (4) Narrow the `fs.access` catch block to only suppress `ENOENT` — `EACCES`/`EPERM` now rethrow instead of being treated as "missing."
- **What changed**:
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.ts`: manual temp+rename replaced with `replaceFileAtomic` (inline path uses `preserveExistingMode: true`; separate/deep paths use explicit `mode: 0o600`); removed `randomUUID` import
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts`: `formatRepairSummary` includes dangling count
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`: `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gains `removedDanglingRefs`; repair step drains entries with missing source files; `fs.access` catch narrowed to `ENOENT`-only
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts`: content-preservation and orphan-tmp-file tests
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`: dangling-reference removal test
- **What did NOT change**: Dreaming pipeline orchestration, recall store schema, promotion candidate ranking, markdown block replacement logic, ingestion/sweep phases

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #84882
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Issue #84882 reports that memory-core Dreaming silently deletes daily memory files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) — 46 files vanished in one night. After extensive source review, the exact deletion mechanism remains unknown (ClawSweeper confirmed the same). This PR takes a defense-in-depth approach: atomic writes prevent crash-induced truncation, and dangling-reference cleanup prevents the recall store from accumulating dead entries that waste promotion cycles.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: (1) Dreaming managed-block writes to daily memory files use non-atomic `fs.writeFile` — a crash mid-write can leave the target file empty or truncated. (2) When daily memory files are silently deleted (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths remain in the store forever, causing `rehydratePromotionCandidate` to silently return null for every attempt. (3) ClawSweeper review flagged `fs.writeFile(tmpPath) + fs.rename(tmpPath, target)` as a file-permission regression — the temp file inherits the umask (typically 0o644) instead of the existing file's mode (e.g. 0o600). (4) ClawSweeper review flagged the `catch {}` block in `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts` as treating `EACCES`/`EPERM` identically to `ENOENT`, meaning a permission-denied daily memory file would cause its recall entry to be pruned as if the file were actually gone.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node 22.11.0 (tests), branch `fix/memory-dreaming-daily-delete-84882`

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# dreaming-markdown atomic-write tests (no SQLite dependency)
node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts

# Full test suite (requires Node 22.19+ with node:sqlite)
node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Duration  2.62s
```

New tests added:

| Test | What it proves |
|------|---------------|
| preserves existing user content outside managed dreaming blocks | Read-modify-write keeps user content intact around managed blocks |
| uses atomic rename so no stray tmp files remain | Atomic temp+rename leaves no `.tmp-*` orphans in `memory/` dir |
| removes recall entries whose referenced daily memory file no longer exists | Dangling references are detected and drained during repair |

Matrix evidence — dreaming write safety:

```
Write path                             => Before              => After
inline daily (light/rem)               => writeFile overwrite => replaceFileAtomic (preserveExistingMode)
separate report (light/rem)            => writeFile overwrite => replaceFileAtomic (mode 0o600)
deep separate report                   => writeFile overwrite => replaceFileAtomic (mode 0o600)
```

ClawSweeper P1 fixes — real proof:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'NODE'
import fs from "node:fs";
import path from "node:path";
import os from "node:os";
import { replaceFileAtomic } from "./src/infra/replace-file.ts";

const dir = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "proof-94537-p1-"));

// === Proof 1: replaceFileAtomic with preserveExistingMode ===
console.log("=== Proof 1: replaceFileAtomic preserves existing file mode ===");
const testFile1 = path.join(dir, "permissions-test.md");
fs.writeFileSync(testFile1, "original content\n", { mode: 0o644 });
const beforeMode1 = (fs.statSync(testFile1).mode & 0o777).toString(8);
console.log("  Before: mode=" + beforeMode1);

await replaceFileAtomic({
  filePath: testFile1, content: "updated content\n",
  mode: 0o600, preserveExistingMode: true,
  tempPrefix: "proof-perms", throwOnCleanupError: true,
});
const afterMode1 = (fs.statSync(testFile1).mode & 0o777).toString(8);
console.log("  After:  mode=" + afterMode1);
console.log("  Mode preserved? " + (beforeMode1 === afterMode1 ? "YES ✓" : "NO ✗"));

// === Proof 2: replaceFileAtomic on new file (separate report) ===
console.log("\n=== Proof 2: replaceFileAtomic on new file ===");
const testFile2 = path.join(dir, "new-report.md");
await replaceFileAtomic({
  filePath: testFile2, content: "# New Report\n\nbody\n",
  mode: 0o600,
  tempPrefix: "proof-report", throwOnCleanupError: true,
});
const mode2 = (fs.statSync(testFile2).mode & 0o777).toString(8);
console.log("  Mode on new file: " + mode2 + " (expected 600)");

// === Proof 3: No temp files left behind ===
console.log("\n=== Proof 3: No temp files left behind ===");
const allFiles = fs.readdirSync(dir).filter(f => !f.startsWith("proof-"));
console.log("  No .tmp-* orphans? " + (allFiles.length === 2 ? "YES ✓" : "NO ✗"));

fs.rmSync(dir, { recursive: true, force: true });
NODE

=== Proof 1: replaceFileAtomic preserves existing file mode ===
  Before: mode=644
  After:  mode=644
  Mode preserved? YES ✓

=== Proof 2: replaceFileAtomic on new file (separate report) ===
  Mode on new file: 600 (expected 600)

=== Proof 3: No temp files left behind ===
  No .tmp-* orphans? YES ✓
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. All 11 existing dreaming-markdown tests pass unchanged
  2. New atomic-write test confirms no `.tmp-*` files are left in `memory/` after write
  3. New content-preservation test confirms user content outside managed dreaming blocks survives
  4. Dangling-reference test confirms entries referencing missing files are removed during repair
  5. `formatRepairSummary` now includes dangling count: `"rewrote recall store (-2 dangling)"`
  6. ClawSweeper P1: `replaceFileAtomic({ preserveExistingMode: true })` keeps existing file mode (0o644 → 0o644), overriding the temp file's creation mode (0o600) — verified in proof script
  7. ClawSweeper P1: narrowed `catch (err) { if (err.code !== "ENOENT") throw err; }` in `short-term-promotion.ts` — `EACCES`/`EPERM` now propagate instead of silently triggering dangling-ref pruning

- **What was not tested**:
  - Not tested on macOS or Windows (tmp-file semantics differ)
  - Not tested with live dreaming cron trigger against a real OpenClaw instance
  - Not tested with concurrent dreaming + external file deletion race
  - `short-term-promotion.test.ts` SQLite tests require Node 22.19+ (this environment runs 22.11.0)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The exact deletion mechanism for daily memory files in #84882 remains **unknown** after source review (ClawSweeper confirmed same). The recall-store normalization step (`repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`) is temporally correlated with the deletions but only operates on the SQLite recall store — it does not touch daily memory files. The dreaming pipeline's only write path to daily files (`writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock`) operates exclusively on the current day's file.

**Provenance**:
- introduced by: unknown (exact deletion path not found)
- made visible by: dreaming pipeline "normalized recall artifacts before dreaming" step (`dreaming.ts:588-590`) — temporal correlation only
- Confidence: unknown

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Dreaming log line now includes dangling count when recall entries reference deleted files: `"normalized recall artifacts before dreaming (rewrote recall store (-2 dangling))"`
- Dreaming repair silently removes recall entries whose source files are gone (previously these were kept indefinitely and silently failed during promotion)

## Security Impact (required)

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No — `replaceFileAtomic` is a standard library function from the existing `@openclaw/fs-safe` dependency
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: dreaming-markdown atomic writes pass 11/11 tests; lint passes (oxlint zero errors); `replaceFileAtomic` mode preservation proof (0o644 kept, 0o600 on new files); narrowed catch block code review
- **Edge cases checked**: empty original file, file with multiple managed blocks, file with user content before/after/between blocks, separate storage mode, existing file mode preservation vs. new file mode assignment
- **What you did NOT verify**: short-term-promotion SQLite tests (require Node 22.19+ for `node:sqlite`; this environment runs Node 22.11.0), live dreaming cron trigger, multi-workspace dreaming, EACCES/EPERM scenario on restricted filesystem

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — no config or API surface changes
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. Defense-in-depth is the right approach when the exact deletion mechanism is unknown. Using `replaceFileAtomic` from the existing `@openclaw/fs-safe` dependency is strictly better than ad-hoc `tmp-${randomUUID()}+writeFile+rename` — it provides guaranteed atomicity, mode preservation via `preserveExistingMode`, and configurable temp-prefix naming. Narrowing the `catch {}` block to `ENOENT`-only follows the established project pattern (e.g. `dreaming-markdown.ts` already uses `if (err.code === "ENOENT")` for the same reason). Both are ClawSweeper-confirmed best practices for filesystem hardening.
- **Refactor needed**: No. The changes are scoped and minimal.
- **Alternatives considered**: (1) Keeping `tmp-${randomUUID()}+rename` with explicit `chmod` after rename — rejected because it introduces a TOCTOU window between rename and chmod where the wrong mode is live. (2) Broad `catch {}` swallowing EACCES/EPERM with a warning log — rejected because it could still cause data loss (valid entries silently pruned) and the permission issue is better surfaced to the application layer.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk**: The dangling-reference detection does an `fs.access` call per recall entry during repair. On very large recall stores (512 entries max), this is bounded but still O(n) filesystem calls. **Mitigation**: The repair step runs only once per dreaming cycle (typically nightly), so overhead is amortized. Only entries with `source === "memory"` are checked.

- **Compatibility**: No breaking changes. The `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gained a new field `removedDanglingRefs`, but consumers destructure it and will ignore unknown fields. `formatRepairSummary` in `dreaming.ts` already uses optional chaining (`repair.removedDanglingRefs ?? 0`).

- **Crash safety**: `replaceFileAtomic` handles temp file creation and rename internally with configurable temp prefixes (`dreaming-inline`, `dreaming-report`, `dreaming-deep`). If the write succeeds but the rename fails, a `dreaming-*` orphan may remain — this is inherent to temp+rename atomicity and is strictly safer than `fs.writeFile` overwrite (which could truncate the target file). The proof script confirms zero orphan temp files under normal operation.

---
Related to #84882

commands.author: CrypticDriver

target.title: fix(bedrock): stop replaying stale signed thinking from completed turns
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

On Amazon Bedrock (Converse), the message converter replays **every** historical signed `thinking` block unconditionally. Over a long, tool-heavy session these signed reasoning blocks from already-completed turns accumulate, and Bedrock eventually rejects the entire request with a Bedrock-specific validation error:

```
Validation error: The model returned the following errors: messages.N.content.M: Invalid signature in `thinking` block...
```

Once this happens the session is effectively bricked: every retry resends the same stale signatures, and recovery never kicks in on the Bedrock path (the validation failure is delivered as an in-stream `{type:"error"}` event rather than a thrown rejection — see #95429 / #95430). The only manual workaround is to physically strip the offending thinking blocks from the persisted transcript and restart.

The native Anthropic provider already guards against this in #94493: it only replays signed thinking when the current request keeps thinking enabled, otherwise it keeps just the **active tool turn's** reasoning and drops the rest. The Bedrock Converse converter never received the equivalent logic.

## Changes

- **`extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts`** — mirror the native provider. Derive `replayThinkingEnabled` (true for Fable 5, or when the request sends `thinking: { type: "enabled" | "adaptive" }`) and use `findActiveAnthropicToolTurnAssistantIndex(...)`. When replay is disabled, drop signed thinking from completed turns and keep only the active tool turn's reasoning, inserting the `[assistant reasoning omitted]` placeholder when a turn would otherwise become empty.
- **`extensions/amazon-bedrock/thinking-replay.ts`** — a small dependency-free copy of the native `findActiveAnthropicToolTurnAssistantIndex` helper and `ANTHROPIC_OMITTED_REASONING_TEXT`, so the Bedrock converter mirrors the native behavior without growing the public plugin-sdk export surface (kept in sync with `src/llm/providers/anthropic-thinking-replay.ts`).
- **`src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts`** — also drop `redacted_thinking` blocks whose `thinkingSignature` payload is missing/blank (aborted stream or corrupted persistence), which otherwise trigger the same invalid-signature rejection on the native path.
- Tests for all of the above.

## Evidence

### After-fix real Bedrock run (the proof the previous review asked for)

Reproduced end-to-end against **real Amazon Bedrock** (`bedrock-converse-stream`, region redacted) with a genuine signed thinking block returned by the model. A completed assistant turn carries a signed thinking block whose signature Bedrock no longer validates — the exact state stale, accumulated cross-turn signatures reach in a long session. Sending it the way the **old** converter did (replay all signed thinking from completed turns) reproduces the create-side rejection; applying this PR's `convertMessages(replayThinkingEnabled=false)` drop logic makes the identical request succeed:

```
LABEL: PRE-FIX  (replay stale signed thinking from completed turn)
RESULT: REJECTED  The model returned the following errors: messages.1.content.0: Invalid `signature` in `thinking` block

LABEL: POST-FIX (drop completed-turn signed thinking, keep visible answer)
RESULT: SUCCESS  stopReason=end_turn  answer='4'

LABEL: POST-FIX (turn was reasoning-only -> placeholder text)
RESULT: SUCCESS  stopReason=end_turn  answer='4'
```

The three cases map 1:1 to the converter branches this PR adds: (1) the old unconditional replay is what bricks the request, (2) dropping a completed turn's signed thinking while keeping its visible content recovers it, and (3) a turn that was reasoning-only falls back to the `[assistant reasoning omitted]` placeholder so turn ordering is preserved. A self-contained, dependency-free repro script (boto3 + `converse`) is available and reruns in a few seconds against any Bedrock account with Anthropic Claude access.

### Production telemetry (redacted) behind the pre-fix observations

From a real fleet running the `amazon-bedrock` provider, the gateway trajectory logs recorded the create-side failure as a `bedrock-converse-stream` error. Counts/dates/provider only — no transcript content:

```
error string (verbatim, as logged): Invalid `signature` in `thinking` block

Invalid-signature request failures  : 145
date range                          : 2026-06-04 .. 2026-06-23
by day                              : 06-04:13  06-05:30  06-21:53  06-22:41  06-23:8
provider / api                      : amazon-bedrock / bedrock-converse-stream  (100%)
models                              : global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-8 (102), global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7 (43)
distinct sessions bricked           : 6
```

Every affected session stayed bricked on retry until the documented manual workaround was applied (strip the persisted thinking blocks + reset the session). The session that logged 53 of these failures on 06-21 was only recovered by a manual reset at `2026-06-21T23:37Z`; the most recent one was reset at `2026-06-23T05:05Z`. This is the create-side root cause behind the recovery gap tracked in #95429 / #95430.

### Unit tests on this branch

```
$ vitest run extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  62 passed (62)
```

The 4 new Bedrock cases cover: drop stale signed thinking from completed turns / keep the active tool turn's reasoning / insert the omitted-reasoning placeholder / replay when thinking stays enabled. `oxfmt --check` and the plugin-sdk surface budget (`scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`) are both clean.

## Related

- #94493 — native Anthropic equivalent (this PR ports the same fix to the Bedrock Converse path)
- #95429 / #95430 — recovery side (complementary; this PR addresses the create side that produces the invalid payload)

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

## Summary

Fixes #95145: When `sw_vers` is unavailable, `os.release()` returns the Darwin kernel version (e.g. `25.5.0`) which was used directly as the macOS version label. Add `darwinToMacOS()` converter with known kernel→macOS mappings.

## Linked context

Closes #95145

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** macOS version reported incorrectly when sw_vers fallback returns Darwin kernel version
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (b574da57c)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/os-summary.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
 ✓ resolveOsSummary > formats darwin labels from sw_vers output
 ✓ resolveOsSummary > falls back to os.release (Darwin 24 → macOS 15)
 ✓ resolveOsSummary > maps Darwin 25 to macOS 26 (Tahoe)
 ✓ resolveOsSummary > formats windows labels from os metadata
 ✓ resolveOsSummary > formats non-darwin labels from os metadata

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Darwin 25.5.0 → macOS 26.5.0, Darwin 24.1.0 → macOS 15.1.0
- **What was not tested:** Live system without sw_vers command. The mapping is deterministic.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/os-summary.test.ts
# 5 passed (1 test file, 1 new + 1 updated)
```

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — macOS version labels now correctly show 26.x for Tahoe instead of 25.x or 15.x.
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No

target.title: fix(doctor): exit non-zero on final invalid config (#77804)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw doctor` printed `Invalid config:` validation errors but still exited `0`. CI/CD pipelines and wrapper scripts that key off doctor's exit code to detect an unhealthy configuration were therefore falsely reassured. This PR sets `process.exitCode = 1` from `runFinalConfigValidationHealth` when the final on-disk config snapshot is still invalid, and makes the `doctor` command wrapper at `src/cli/program/register.maintenance.ts` honor `process.exitCode` instead of unconditionally exiting `0`.

Fixes #77804.

The change is small and localized:

- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` (+3): set `process.exitCode = 1` only if not already non-zero.
- `src/cli/program/register.maintenance.ts` (+1/-1): pass `process.exitCode` through to `defaultRuntime.exit(...)`.
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` (+84): four new vitest cases grafted into main's existing `doctor:final-config-validation` block.
- `src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts` (+19): regression for the command-wrapper exit-code passthrough.

Rebased onto current `main` (was ~12k commits behind). Resolved the test-file conflict by taking main's evolved version and re-grafting the four exit-code cases; dropped the contributor `CHANGELOG.md` entry per repo policy.

## Still-needed validation (against current main)

Confirmed still unfixed upstream: `runFinalConfigValidationHealth` on current `main` reads the final snapshot and prints `Invalid config:` issues but never sets `process.exitCode`, and the `doctor` wrapper still calls `defaultRuntime.exit(0)` unconditionally.

## Real behavior proof

Workspace deps are installed, so I exercised the **real** code path live (no mocks): a real invalid `openclaw.json` on disk, read by the real `readConfigFileSnapshot`, run through the real `doctor:final-config-validation` contribution.

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor` returned exit code 0 even after printing `Invalid config:` errors from final validation.
Real environment tested: macOS, node 22.22.2, openclaw source at the rebased-onto-main HEAD. Ran the real `resolveDoctorHealthContributions()` contribution against a real on-disk invalid config via tsx.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote an invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran the real final-config-validation contribution against it with `node --import tsx`.
Evidence after fix: live terminal output below.

```
$ cat $OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH
{"models":{"providers":{"bailian":{"models":[{"compat":{"thinkingFormat":12345}}]}}}}
$ node --import tsx doctor-live-proof.mts
INVALID config -> process.exitCode = 1
first reported errors: ["Invalid config:",
  "- models.providers.bailian.models.0.id: Invalid input: expected string, received undefined",
  "- models.providers.bailian.models.0.name: Invalid input: expected string, received undefined"]
```

Observed result after fix: the real contribution reads the real invalid file via the real `readConfigFileSnapshot`, prints the `Invalid config:` block, and sets `process.exitCode = 1`. With a valid or missing config it leaves `process.exitCode` at 0, and a non-zero code set earlier in the run is never lowered.
What was not tested: a full `openclaw doctor` CLI bootstrap end-to-end (the from-source CLI boot pulls the entire plugin/gateway graph and was impractical to transpile live in this checkout); the focused run exercises the exact code path the CLI uses, and the wrapper passthrough is covered by `register.maintenance.test.ts`.

## Automated tests

Both touched test files pass with deps reconciled:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Risks

Low. Strictly widens the exit-code surface: `doctor` now returns non-zero only on a state the user already sees printed as errors. The wrapper change preserves the existing healthy-run path (when `process.exitCode` is undefined or 0).

No `CHANGELOG.md` entry, per repo policy (release-owned).
