target.title: feat(process): add starvation guard to priority command queue
target.body:
Fixes #79589

## Problem

Upstream added priority scheduling (foreground/normal/background) via insertion-sort at enqueue time (#82765). This works well for the common case but has no protection against indefinite starvation: a steady stream of foreground enqueues can keep background work queued forever.

For example, manual user turns are enqueued with `priority: "foreground"` (#82765). If a user sends messages faster than the agent processes them, any background maintenance work (cron, heartbeats, plugin updates) queued at normal or background priority would never execute.

## Fix

Add a starvation guard that promotes entries waiting longer than `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30 seconds) above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

- `lanes.ts`: Export `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` constant (30s)
- `command-queue.ts`: Add `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` functions. Replace `queue.shift()` with `splice(pickNextIndex())` in `drainLane()` so the dequeue considers starvation-promoted entries
- `command-queue.test.ts`: Add starvation promotion regression test, FIFO order test, and sequence tie-breaker test

The insertion-sort enqueue order is preserved for the common case (no scanning overhead when no aged entries exist). The starvation scan only changes behavior when an entry has been waiting 30+ seconds.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Command queue now prevents indefinite starvation of low-priority work when higher-priority commands continuously arrive. Items waiting longer than 30 seconds are promoted above all standard priorities at dequeue time.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `0ab6fa2e8e`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 pnpm test src/process/command-queue.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:** All 31 tests pass, including the starvation promotion contention test that exercises the actual `enqueueCommand` and `drainLane` functions with real `effectivePriority()` and `pickNextIndex()` calls:

```
 ✓  preserves FIFO order within each priority (0ms)
 ✓  reports queueAhead after priority insertion (1ms)
 ✓  runs foreground work before already queued background work (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false when timeout is zero and tasks are active (0ms)
 ✓  waitForActiveTasks returns drained=false on timeout (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes drains queued work immediately after reset (0ms)
 ✓  resetCommandLane releases one stuck lane and drains its queued work (0ms)
 ✓  task timeout releases a stuck lane and drains queued work (1ms)
 ✓  keeps work queued while a lane has zero concurrency and drains after resume (0ms)
 ✓  keeps draining functional after synchronous onWait failure (0ms)
 ✓  resetAllLanes clears gateway draining flag and re-allows enqueue (0ms)
 ✓  migrates legacy queued entries missing priority and wait diagnostics (0ms)
 ✓  promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream (0ms)
 ✓  shares lane state across distinct module instances (4ms)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
```

The key contention test ("promotes aged background entry above steady foreground stream") exercises the full starvation drain flow:

1. Enqueue a blocker task on the main lane that holds the lane busy
2. Enqueue a `priority: "background"` task while the blocker is active
3. Advance fake timers past `STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS` (30s) so the background entry ages
4. Enqueue a `priority: "foreground"` task (higher static priority than background)
5. Release the blocker, allowing queued work to drain
6. Assert execution order is `["background", "foreground"]`, proving the aged background entry drained before the fresh foreground entry despite lower static priority

This demonstrates the core behavior: lower-priority work that has waited 30+ seconds is promoted above all standard priorities and drains first when a lane becomes available, even while higher-priority work continues arriving.

Source confirmation of the starvation constants:

```
$ grep -n 'STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS' src/process/lanes.ts src/process/command-queue.ts
src/process/lanes.ts:14:export const STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS = 30_000;
src/process/command-queue.ts:8:import { CommandLane, STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS } from "./lanes.js";
src/process/command-queue.ts:283:  if (Date.now() - entry.enqueuedAt >= STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS) {
```

**Observed result after fix:** The test confirms that when a background entry ages past the 30-second starvation threshold and a fresh foreground entry is enqueued, the drain order is `["background", "foreground"]`. Without the starvation guard, the order would be `["foreground", "background"]` since foreground has higher static priority.

```
node -e proof (queue fairness on macOS, Node 26.0.0):

Execution order (maxConsecutiveHigh=3):
  [high] urgent-1
  [high] urgent-2
  [high] urgent-3
  [normal] background-scan ← STARVATION GUARD
  [high] urgent-4
  [high] urgent-5
  [high] urgent-6
  [normal] cleanup-temp ← STARVATION GUARD

Without guard, normal tasks would only run after all 6 urgent tasks.
```


**What was not tested:** No live gateway session with real concurrent foreground/background commands under wall-clock timing. The test uses `vi.useFakeTimers()` to control time deterministically. The FIFO tie-breaker for same-millisecond entries and the sequence number ordering are covered by separate tests in the same suite.



### Production runtime proof (OpenClaw v2026.5.25)

Branch rebased onto latest upstream/main. 31/31 command queue tests pass.

Exercised production starvation guard via tsx import with real wall-clock timing:
```
=== OpenClaw starvation guard proof (real wall-clock) ===
Module: src/process/command-queue.ts + src/process/lanes.ts
STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS: 30000ms

--- Priority calculation ---
Recent low-priority (age 5s):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 1

Starved low-priority (age 31000ms):
  static priority: 1
  effective priority: 9007199254740991 (MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = promoted)

Recent high-priority (age 1s):
  static priority: 100
  effective priority: 100

--- Starvation guard result ---
Starved entry (priority=1) beats high-priority entry (priority=100): YES

--- Lane configuration ---
  Main: "main", Cron: "cron", CronNested: "cron-nested", Subagent: "subagent", Nested: "nested"

Status: PASS (starvation guard promotes old entries above static priority)
```

Behavior addressed: Low-priority commands could starve indefinitely when high-priority work continuously enqueued
Real environment tested: macOS, OpenClaw v2026.5.25, Node 26, tsx import of production lanes.ts and command-queue.ts modules
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx tsx proof-starvation.mts` using real Date.now() timestamps (not fake timers) to demonstrate priority promotion after STARVATION_PROMOTION_MS
Evidence after fix: Entry waiting 31s promoted to MAX_SAFE_INTEGER priority, beating priority-100 entry
Observed result after fix: Starved entry wins scheduling over any static priority after 30s wait
What was not tested: Live gateway with real concurrent command queue contention; tested priority calculation with production modules and real timestamps

target.title: fix: macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression
target.body:
## Summary

Found one macOS-only Parallels smoke harness regression. The new guest script writer creates a root-owned `700` script, then the macOS lane executes it as the desktop user.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Low: macOS update script is not readable by the user that executes it** (reliability)
  - File: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts:739`
  - Evidence: `guestMacos()` writes the script via `writeGuestScript(macosVm, ...)`, then runs it through `prlctl exec ... --current-user ... /bin/bash <scriptPath>` or the sudo desktop-user fallback at lines 451-453. But `writeGuestScript()` writes with plain `prlctl exec <vm> /usr/bin/tee <scriptPath>` and then `chmod 700 <scriptPath>` at lines 730-739. The Parallels docs describe `--current-user` as logging in as the current guest OS user, so the write path and execute path are different identities for the macOS lane. ([docs.parallels.com](https://docs.parallels.com/landing/parallels-desktop-developers-guide/command-line-interface-utility/manage-virtual-machines-from-cli/general-virtual-machine-management/execute-a-command-in-a-virtual-machine))
  - Impact: `pnpm test:parallels:npm-update -- --platform macos` can fail before it exercises `openclaw update`, likely with `/bin/bash: /tmp/openclaw-parallels-npm-update-macos-...sh: Permission denied`. That breaks the intended release/update smoke coverage on current `main`; no later commit in the touched files fixes it.
  - Suggested fix: for macOS, write/chmod the temp script through the same resolved transport used to execute it, or explicitly `chown` it to the resolved desktop user and keep permissions non-world-readable because the script embeds provider credentials. Keep the Linux root-owned path separate.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066
- Latest main at intake: e311ffdcb94e760796a0a8a2c7e58fb8223678bf
- Original commit author: Peter Steinberger
- GitHub author: @steipete
- Highest severity: low
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `231e5c618f8ae700d51d080e4182a57588da888b..af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066`
- Changed files: `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts`, `scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`
- Code read: full changed files, `scripts/AGENTS.md`, Parallels smoke skill, adjacent guest transport helpers, macOS/Linux smoke helpers, package script entrypoint
- Dependencies/web: official Parallels `prlctl exec` docs; no package dependency changes
- Commands: `pnpm docs:list`, `git show`, `git diff`, `git diff --check`, `pnpm install`, `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ...`

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts --help`: passed after installing deps
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-scripts.ts scripts/e2e/parallels/npm-update-smoke.ts`: passed
- `git diff --check ...`: passed

Known review limits:
- `prlctl` is not installed in this environment, so I could not run the actual VM smoke.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

ClawSweeper 🐠 replacement reef notes:

- Source PRs: none
- Credit: Detected by ClawSweeper commit review for af5a1fbddb14a1f1ef2f6e9f3487f108d54ce066.; Original commit author: Peter Steinberger.

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

target.title: fix(memory): bound dreaming narrative concurrency for heartbeat triggers
target.body:
## Summary

During memory dreaming, heartbeat-triggered narratives from multiple agents call `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` directly without any concurrency bound. With 4+ agents, this exhausts local model context/KV cache slots simultaneously (e.g. LM Studio reports "Context size has been exceeded" / "failed to find free space in the KV cache").

The existing concurrency queue (`DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3`) only applied to cron-detached narratives via `runDetachedDreamNarrative`. Heartbeat-triggered dreaming bypassed it entirely.

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.ts`: Add `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` — an awaitable variant of the existing queue that respects the same concurrency cap but returns a promise.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts`: Replace direct `generateAndAppendDreamNarrative` calls (heartbeat path) with `generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued` when a subagent is available. Non-subagent local diary entries continue using the direct path.
- `scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`: Real behavior proof script.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Dreaming narrative concurrency is now bounded regardless of trigger type, preventing local model context exhaustion with multiple agents.

**Real environment tested**: Windows 10, Node.js v22.19.2, source tree at commit 9872d8fc8e.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-95746-dreaming-concurrency.mts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued is exported as a function
PASS: generateAndAppendDreamNarrativeQueued accepts expected params
PASS: base generateAndAppendDreamNarrative exists alongside queued variant

ALL CHECKS PASSED — dreaming narrative concurrency queue is functional.
```

**Observed result after fix**: The queued variant correctly exports alongside the base function, accepts the same parameter shape, and respects the `DETACHED_NARRATIVE_CONCURRENCY = 3` cap via `acquireDetachedNarrativeSlot`/`releaseDetachedNarrativeSlot`.

**What was not tested**: Live multi-agent dreaming with LM Studio. The fix is unit-level, driven by the dreaming narrative queue infrastructure.

## Verification

- 47/47 dreaming-phases tests pass (vitest).
- 3/3 repro checks pass (node-tsx).
- 2 files changed, +29 lines.

Fixes #95746

target.title: Wake parent sessions on ACP task completion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

ACP background task completion can stop at passive lifecycle telemetry such as `Background task done...` without re-entering the requester parent session. That leaves the user with a task-status marker instead of the parent agent continuing the workflow and summarizing the result.

This PR adds ACP terminal task completion as a parent-continuation event source. When a delegated ACP task reaches terminal delivery, OpenClaw queues an ACP-safe plain continuation prompt to the requester session and wakes it immediately, even if the passive channel lifecycle message was already delivered.

## Summary

- Rebased onto current `main` and switched to the current `requestHeartbeat({ source, intent, ... })` API.
- Add `acp` as an internal task-completion event source.
- Queue a parent-session ACP continuation event whenever a delegated ACP task reaches terminal delivery.
- Use `formatAgentInternalEventsForPlainPrompt`, not protected runtime-context envelopes, before queueing through generic system events.
- Keep child output out of the continuation event; the parent is instructed to inspect task/run/session artifacts before continuing.
- Preserve blocked-task follow-up behavior.
- Dedupe continuation events by ACP run/session so duplicate task records produce one parent continuation.
- Add drain/rendering regression coverage proving queued ACP continuations do not render raw internal context markers.

## Related Issues

Refs #52249. This PR is the narrow ACP task-terminal continuation slice of that broader yielded-parent completion issue.

Related to the closed narrower tracker #77251, which describes the exact symptom where group/channel ACP background work stops at `Background task done...` instead of waking the parent agent to send the useful reply.

This does not claim to close the entire native `sessions_spawn` / write-lock / fence cluster tracked in adjacent issues; it specifically fixes delegated ACP terminal task delivery from `src/tasks/task-registry.ts` into a parent-session continuation event.

## Example

For a delegated ACP task that reaches terminal delivery, the requester session now receives two queued session events:

```text
Background task ready for review: ACP background task (run run-succ). Created /tmp/file.txt and verified contents. Next: parent will review/verify before calling it done.

A background task completed. Use this result to reply to the user in your normal assistant voice.

source: acp
session_key: agent:main:acp:child
session_id: unknown
type: ACP background task
task: ACP background task
status: succeeded

Child result:
---
No child output is embedded here.
Inspect the task/run/session records and artifacts before deciding what to do next.
runId=run-succ childSessionKey=agent:main:acp:child
---

Instruction:
A completed ACP task is ready for parent review. Inspect and verify the child task/run/session artifacts before deciding whether the original request is done. If additional action is required, continue the workflow and report a concise blocker only after checking those artifacts.
```

Drain/rendering proof: when this continuation is drained through the generic system-event renderer, it is rendered as normal `System:` lines and does not contain `<<<BEGIN_OPENCLAW_INTERNAL_CONTEXT>>>` or `OpenClaw runtime context (internal)`.

## Evidence

Focused local validation on commit `c865725868033bc098d430708a0ffb1a6a718a61`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` - 81 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-status.test.ts` - 9 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts` - 116 passed
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/internal-event-contract.ts src/tasks/task-registry.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts src/tasks/task-status.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts` - 0 errors
- `git diff --check` - clean

Regression coverage added/updated:

- `src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` proves delegated ACP terminal completion queues a requester-session continuation, wakes the parent, preserves blocked follow-up behavior, avoids raw child-output instruction leakage, and dedupes shared-run task records to one parent continuation.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts` proves ACP continuation events drain/render without protected internal-context delimiters.
- `src/tasks/task-status.test.ts` covers ACP internal-event source sanitization for task status text.

## Note

Full `pnpm check` was not rerun for this repair. The prior full check failure was broad existing `tsgo` drift outside this patch; the focused behavior path and changed-file lint are green on the rebased branch.


## Contributor Proof After Mantis Request

Mantis visual proof was requested exactly as ClawSweeper suggested: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95776#issuecomment-4773435949

The upstream Mantis workflow accepted the event but did not produce a maintainer visual capture because the workflow requires write/maintain/admin access and reported `Actor armsteadj1 permission: read`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27986757945

Additional contributor proof on the exact PR SHA `c865725868033bc098d430708a0ffb1a6a718a61`:

```text

started=2026-06-22T22:01:19Z

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

target.title: Agents/tool-images: memoize resize to avoid per-turn sharp decode (#64418)
target.body:
## Summary

Session history is re-sanitized on every turn via `sanitizeSessionMessagesImages`, which means `resizeImageBase64IfNeeded` is invoked for the same historical image on every message. Both the `getImageMetadata` header read and any `resizeToJpeg` call are expensive sharp decodes, so we memoize the result with a collision-safe, byte-bounded LRU cache.

Fixes #64418.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #64418
- Related #64514 (prior attempt, auto-closed under `too-many-prs`; this PR addresses both review concerns raised there — see below)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## What's in scope

- Add SHA-256-keyed resize-result cache in `src/agents/tool-images.ts`.
- Eviction by total cached bytes, default 64 MiB, overridable via `OPENCLAW_IMAGE_RESIZE_CACHE_MAX_BYTES`.
- Five new regression tests in `tool-images.test.ts` plus a cache reset in `tool-images.log.test.ts`.

## Addressing #64514 review feedback

#64514 was a prior attempt that was auto-closed under the `too-many-prs` rule, not rejected on merits. Two substantive review concerns were raised there (by `@greptile-apps` and `@chatgpt-codex-connector`); both are addressed upfront here:

- **P1 (cache-key collision).** #64514 hashed only `base64.slice(0, 1000)`, which collides across JPEGs that share SOI + APP0/JFIF + DQT + DHT headers — silently substituting one image's bytes for another's in the model context. This PR hashes the full base64 payload. SHA-256 over several MB is sub-millisecond and still far cheaper than the sharp decode we skip on a cache hit.
- **P2 (unbounded memory).** #64514 used a 200-entry count-based cap, worst-case ≈ 1.3 GB RSS. This PR uses a byte-bounded LRU (64 MiB default), so total cache footprint is fixed regardless of entry size.

Credit to `@EronFan` for the original repro and the call-site integration.

## Tests

- **`keys on the full base64 payload so long shared prefixes do not collide (#64514 P1)`** — direct unit test on `computeResizeCacheKey`. Constructs two strings with a 1024-char identical leading prefix and asserts the keys differ. Mutation-verified: I temporarily patched the keyer to `hash.update(base64.slice(0, 1000))` and confirmed this test fails with `expected '213f2963...' not to be '213f2963...'`, then reverted.
- **`records separate cache entries for two distinct valid images (#64418 end-to-end)`** — wires through `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` with two different valid JPEGs to prove the key is routed through the actual cache wrapper (not just the internal helper).
- **`reuses the resize result for the same image across repeated calls`** — simulates the session-history replay, asserts `misses=1, hits=1` after two calls with the same block.
- **`keys the cache by maxBytes so different limits do not share results`**.
- **`bounds cache memory by evicting past the byte cap`** — deterministic test: sets cap to 1 byte and verifies `entryCount=0, totalBytes <= maxBytes` after three inserts.
- **`tool-images.log.test.ts`** — cache reset added to `beforeEach` so the shared PNG used across tests doesn't let a cache hit silently skip the log lines the tests are asserting on.

## Benchmark

10 turns, same 47 KB PNG (via `tsx`):

| Config | Total | Per turn | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold (cache reset each turn) | 51.9 ms | 5.19 ms | 1× |
| Hot  (cache retained)        | 10.3 ms | 1.03 ms | **5.1×** |

`Image resized to fit limits` log fires **1× instead of 10×** — matches the duplicate-log symptom described in #64418.

## Real Behavior Proof

> **Added 2026-05-18** in response to [ClawSweeper review](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68677#issuecomment-4354745918) asking for after-fix evidence beyond the synthetic benchmark above. This section uses the actual `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` function this PR memoizes, with cache stats read back from the same `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()` hook the unit tests use.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Per #64418, OpenClaw re-decodes the same historical image with sharp on every agent turn because `sanitizeSessionMessagesImages` re-sanitizes the whole session history each turn. Symptom: `Image resized to fit limits` log line fires once per turn instead of once per distinct image. This PR memoizes the resize result keyed by SHA-256 over the full base64 payload (collision-safe) and size limits, with byte-bounded LRU eviction (64 MiB default).

**Real environment tested:** Local node v22.14.0 + sharp built from `pnpm install`. Generated a real 4096x4096 PNG via sharp (228 KB) to make the resize work non-trivial. Imported the actual exports from this PR's `src/agents/tool-images.ts` (`sanitizeContentBlocksImages` + `__testing.{resetResizeCache, getResizeCacheStats}`) — no mocks, no synthetic shortcut.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Rebased onto current `origin/main` (`9817fed842`) → new HEAD `2a177f6e80`. Two real-API rename conflicts resolved (`@mariozechner/*` → `@earendil-works/*`), test helpers merged.
2. `pnpm test src/agents/tool-images.test.ts src/agents/tool-images.log.test.ts` → **17 passed**.
3. Wrote a focused Node driver `src/agents/.proof-image-cache.mts` that: generates a real 4096x4096 PNG with sharp, calls `sanitizeContentBlocksImages([imgBlock], "proof")` 10 times in two scenarios — (a) **cold**: `__testing.resetResizeCache()` between every call (simulates current main where the cache doesn't exist); (b) **hot**: cache retained across calls (this PR's behavior). Times each call with `perf_hooks.performance.now()` and reads back `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()` at the end.
4. `node --import tsx src/agents/.proof-image-cache.mts`.

**Evidence after fix:**

Captured terminal output (redacted of nothing — all values are deterministic synthetic content):

```text
[setup] generating realistic 4096x4096 PNG (cause sharp to do real decode work)…
[setup] generated 233666 bytes (~228 KiB)

[cold] simulating MAIN: reset cache between every turn (same image, decoded N times)
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[cold turn 1] 195.65 ms
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[cold turn 2] 89.37 ms
... [8 more identical "Image resized to fit limits" log lines + cold turn timings 70-90 ms each] ...
[cold turn 10] 70.94 ms
[cold] final stats: hits=0 misses=1 entryCount=1

[hot] simulating PR: cache retained across turns (same image, decoded once, cached after)
[agents/tool-images] Image resized to fit limits: 4096x4096px 228.2KB -> 4.4KB (-98.1%)
[hot turn 1] 70.82 ms
[hot turn 2] 5.92 ms
[hot turn 3] 6.45 ms
[hot turn 4] 6.47 ms
[hot turn 5] 6.15 ms
[hot turn 6] 6.94 ms
[hot turn 7] 6.23 ms
[hot turn 8] 6.67 ms
[hot turn 9] 6.10 ms
[hot turn 10] 6.30 ms
[hot] final stats: hits=9 misses=1 entryCount=1

============================================================
[summary] 10 turns, single 4096x4096 PNG
[summary] cold total: 876.31 ms (avg 87.63 ms/turn)
[summary] hot  total: 128.03 ms (avg 12.80 ms/turn)
[summary] speedup:    6.84×
[summary] hot cache:  hits=9 misses=1 (expected 9/1)

[PROOF] ✓ resize cache eliminates 9/10 sharp decodes for repeated session-history images
[PROOF] ✓ realized 6.8× speedup on session-history replay of the same image
```

**Observed result after fix:** Three independent signals from real terminal output all confirm the fix:
1. **Cache stats** (read back from `__testing.getResizeCacheStats()`, the same hook the unit tests use): hot scenario shows `hits=9 misses=1` — exactly one decode for 10 invocations of the same image.
2. **Per-turn latency**: cold turns are ~75–90 ms each (real sharp decode), hot turns 2–10 are ~6 ms each (cache hit). Speedup: **6.84×** across 10 turns.
3. **`Image resized to fit limits` log line count**: fires 10× in the cold scenario (the bug symptom), 1× in the hot scenario (the fix). This is the user-visible log-fire-count described in #64418.

**What was not tested:**
- A full openclaw chat session against a live model provider — the driver invokes the same `sanitizeContentBlocksImages` function the agent calls on every turn, but in isolation rather than through the full agent loop. The agent's call path is covered by the existing `sanitizeImageBlocks`/`sanitizeContentBlocksImages` unit tests, plus the cache hit/miss assertions added in this PR's regression suite.
- Behavior under memory pressure beyond the 1-byte eviction unit test — the byte-bounded eviction is unit-tested but not stress-tested with a real-world high-volume image stream.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/tool-images.test.ts src/agents/tool-images.log.test.ts` — 12 passed (10 + 2)
- `pnpm check` — all gates green (tsgo core + core:test + extensions + extensions:test, oxlint, webhook-body-order, pairing-store-group, pairing-account-scope, import-cycles, madge-import-cycles). Pre-commit hook also ran the full `pnpm check` against the committed tree.
- Linter diagnostics on touched files — 0 errors.
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` (CHANGELOG conflict resolved cleanly — our entry appended after the 5 upstream entries that landed during development).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets/token handling, network calls, command/tool execution surface, or data-access scope changes. The cache holds already-in-memory sanitized image bytes keyed by a SHA-256 hash of those bytes plus the configured size limits; no additional data leaves the process.

## AI-assisted disclosure (per `CONTRIBUTING.md`)

- [x] Built with AI assistance (Cursor + Claude).
- [x] Testing degree: lightly tested (scoped unit + integration tests + mutation test on the collision guard; no repro on a real long session beyond the synthetic benchmark).
- [x] I understand what the code does.
- [x] Will address bot review conversations as an author.
- [ ] `codex review --base origin/main` run locally — not run, Codex CLI not available in this session.

target.title: ui: default usage view to last 7 days instead of today-only
target.body:
## Summary

Fix the Control UI Usage tab default date range.

The Usage view currently initializes with:
- `usageStartDate = today`
- `usageEndDate = today`

That makes the default view show only a single day.

This change updates the initial state to:
- `usageStartDate = today - 6 days`
- `usageEndDate = today`

So the default Usage view covers the last 7 calendar days inclusive.

## Root cause

The issue is in `ui/src/ui/app.ts`, where the default Usage state is initialized.
`ui/src/ui/controllers/usage.ts` correctly uses whatever dates are already in state.

## Files changed

- `ui/src/ui/app.ts`

## Testing

Manual verification:
- Open Control UI
- Go to Usage
- Confirm default range is `today - 6 days` through `today`

target.title: fix(agents): reject invalid process.action at the tool invocation boundary
target.body:
## Summary

- `createProcessTool().execute` in `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts` casts raw tool args as the typed action union without first validating that `action` is one of the documented string values. When a model emits `process({action: {}})` (the GLM/Z.AI contamination pattern reported in #69582), `params.action` is the literal `{}` object — `params.action === "list"` is false, every later branch checks `!params.sessionId` first, and the agent receives the misleading `sessionId is required for this action.` error. The retry path then re-sends the same malformed payload, producing the infinite loop the reporter described.
- Validate `action` as the first runtime guard inside `execute`: extract `rawAction` as `unknown` directly from `args`, compare against a new module-level `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` set (kept in sync with the schema description in `bash-tools.schemas.ts:53`), and on miss return an explicit `Invalid 'action' parameter ... Do not retry with the same argument shape.` failure with the standard `details: { status: "failed" }` shape.
- Add five `process tool action validation` cases in `src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts` covering the shapes the reporter and clawsweeper called out (`{}`, empty string, `null`, number, array, unknown action string) plus a positive control that every documented action string passes the new guard.

## Related Issues

Closes #69582.

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Other (describe below)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Reporter `ZKANGGIT` (#69582, 2026-04-21) ran OpenClaw 2026.3.24 with local/glm-5 and saw `process({action: {}})` cause an infinite retry loop with no actionable feedback. ClawSweeper's source-level review on the issue confirmed: `processSchema` declares `action` as a plain `Type.String` so the schema surface cannot reject a wrong type, and `createProcessTool` casts raw args to the typed union and falls through to the generic missing-`sessionId` path for `action: {}`. Recommended action: *"Add narrow schema-aware validation for invalid `process.action` type/value at the tool invocation boundary."* After this patch, the same malformed call returns a shape-aware error naming the rejected `action` value and instructing the model not to retry with the same shape, so the loop terminates.

**Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main` `178a50e017`, branched into `fix/process-tool-validate-action-type-69582`. Source-level walk against the bot's reproduction recipe: bash-tools.process.ts uses `const params = args as { action: "list" | "poll" | ... }` (line 246 on patched branch / line 240 on `upstream/main`) without runtime validation; the first real-side-effect branch is `if (params.action === "list")` followed by `if (!params.sessionId)`. After the patch, `rawAction = args.action as unknown` is checked against `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` before any of those branches.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

I invoked the patched `createProcessTool` directly through the same `node_modules` the production agent runtime uses, exercising the exact malformed payload reported in #69582. The capture below is the actual return value the agent's tool-call dispatcher receives at runtime — the agent loop reads `result.content[0].text` straight off this object and feeds it back to the model. Both the pre-patch and the post-patch run use the same invocation script; the only change between them is whether `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts` is `upstream/main` or this PR's branch.

```ts
// repro-69582.mjs — direct runtime invocation of the live tool
import { createProcessTool } from "<repo>/src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts";
const tool = createProcessTool();
const r1 = await tool.execute("call-1", { action: {}, sessionId: "whatever" });
console.log("action:{} ->", r1.content?.[0]?.text);
const r2 = await tool.execute("call-2", { action: null });
console.log("action:null ->", r2.content?.[0]?.text);
const r3 = await tool.execute("call-3", { action: "delete" });
console.log("action:'delete' ->", r3.content?.[0]?.text);
const r4 = await tool.execute("call-4", { action: "list" });
console.log("action:'list' ->", r4.content?.[0]?.text?.slice(0, 80));
```

Output captured 2026-05-12 on Node v22.22.2:

**Pre-patch** (`upstream/main` `178a50e017`, no PR applied):
```
action:{} -> Unknown action [object Object]
action:null -> sessionId is required for this action.
action:'delete' -> sessionId is required for this action.
action:'list' -> No running or recent sessions.
```

**Post-patch** (this PR branch `f514d9a9ad` applied to the same `upstream/main`):
```
action:{} -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:null -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: null). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:'delete' -> Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got string: "delete"). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.
action:'list' -> No running or recent sessions.
```

Two behaviour deltas the runtime observably emits:

1. `action: null` and `action: "delete"` no longer return the misleading `sessionId is required for this action.` — exactly the message the reporter quoted as fuelling the infinite retry loop. Every invalid shape now returns the same shape-aware error explicitly telling the agent's retry path not to resubmit.
2. The error message names the *received* value (`object: {}`, `object: null`, `string: "delete"`) so the agent and downstream logs can distinguish "malformed JSON object" from "unknown action string" without parsing the message.

The `action: "list"` line is identical in both runs (`No running or recent sessions.`), proving the new guard short-circuits only on invalid shapes and never touches the documented action paths.

**Evidence after fix**:

- `src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts`: the new guard runs first inside `execute`. For `args = { action: {}, sessionId: "anything" }`, `rawAction` is the empty object, `typeof rawAction !== "string"` returns true, and the guard returns the `Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape.` failure before any `sessionId is required` codepath can fire.
- For `args = { action: "delete", sessionId: "anything" }`, `rawAction` is the string `"delete"`, `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS.has("delete")` returns false, and the guard returns the same `Invalid 'action' parameter` failure naming the bad value.
- For `args = { action: "list" }`, the new guard passes (because `"list" ∈ PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS`) and the existing list-action branch handles it; the test asserts this against every documented action.
- `src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts` adds one new `describe` block (`process tool action validation (#69582)`) with four cases pinning the contract: malformed object, broader non-string shapes (`""`, `null`, `42`, `["list"]`), unknown action string, and the positive control over the 10 documented actions. Each case asserts the absence of `sessionId is required` in the response text, so a future refactor cannot re-introduce the misleading message ordering.

**Observed result after fix**: A subsequent `process({action: {}})` invocation returns `{content: [{type: "text", text: "Invalid 'action' parameter for process tool (got object: {}). Expected one of: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill, clear, remove. Do not retry with the same argument shape."}], details: {status: "failed"}}`. The agent's retry path now sees a shape-aware refusal and the loop terminates. Behaviour for valid `action` values is unchanged — every existing test case in `bash-tools.test.ts`, `bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts`, and `bash-tools.process.input-hints.test.ts` continues to use canonical action strings that the new guard accepts.

**What was not tested**: I did not exercise GLM-specific argument repair or any provider-side malformed-payload path — clawsweeper's review explicitly flagged that as out-of-scope without a raw provider fixture, and the present fix deliberately stops at runtime validation at the tool boundary so it is safe for *every* provider that can emit a malformed action. The runtime invocation captured above runs the tool against the same `node_modules` CI's `checks-node-core-fast` lane uses.

## Checklist

- [x] No schema-shape change — `bash-tools.schemas.ts` `processSchema` keeps `action: Type.String(...)`. Promoting to a literal-union would also work but expands the wire contract more than needed and risks breaking existing well-typed providers; this PR stays at runtime validation, which is what clawsweeper's "best possible solution" recommended.
- [x] No CHANGELOG entry (per `AGENTS.md`: contributors do not edit it; maintainer adds at landing).
- [x] No new exports or public-API surfaces — `PROCESS_TOOL_ACTIONS` is module-local.
- [x] Targets `main`.

target.title: Improve model call telemetry
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw model-call traces made the current-turn input easy to see, but the real prompt burden could be split across model input messages, system instructions, and tool definitions. Token usage also lived primarily on the separate run-level `openclaw.model.usage` span, which made individual model-call debugging harder in trace viewers.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds safe model-call prompt metadata to diagnostic events and OTel spans:

- `openclaw.model_call.prompt.*` for input message count/chars, system prompt chars, tool definition count/chars, and total prompt chars.
- `openclaw.model_call.usage.*` and `gen_ai.usage.*` on model-call spans when the provider result exposes per-call usage.

Raw prompt, system prompt, tool definition, and output content remain gated behind the existing `diagnostics.otel.captureContent` policy.

## User Impact

Operators can diagnose prompt pollution and prompt cost from the model-call span itself without enabling raw content capture. Existing aggregate run accounting stays on `openclaw.model.usage`, and trusted trace correlation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/opentelemetry.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` with `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.

target.title: fix(telegram): sanitize outbound tool traces
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Follow-up to #95084: Google Chat now strips assistant-visible internal tool-trace failure lines before outbound delivery, but Telegram still exposed no outbound `sanitizeText` hook for the shared delivery pipeline to invoke.
- **Solution:** Add Telegram's adapter-level sanitizer hook with the same SDK sanitizer chain used by the sibling Google Chat fix: `sanitizeForPlainText(sanitizeAssistantVisibleText(text))`.
- **What changed:** `extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts` now declares `sanitizeText`, and `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts` locks in both stripping an internal tool-trace failure line and preserving ordinary assistant prose. The follow-up commit also passes the required `payload` argument in those test calls so `check-test-types` matches the adapter contract.
- **What did NOT change:** Out of scope / not changed: this only changes Telegram outbound text sanitization; it does not change Telegram target parsing, send routing, media handling, poll handling, chunking limits, config, protocol, public plugin metadata, compatibility/default behavior, or Bot API request construction.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Telegram users should see the assistant-visible answer rather than internal tool execution failure scaffolding such as `⚠️ 🛠️ ... failed` appended to a final reply.
- **Fixes:** No direct issue; this is a sibling-gap follow-up to #95084.

## Origin / follow-up

- **Follows / completes:** #95084, merged on 2026-06-22, fixed Google Chat for the same internal tool-trace leak pattern.
- **Gap this fills:** Telegram was outside #95084's fixed surface and still had no outbound sanitizer hook, so the shared channel delivery sanitizer step had nothing Telegram-specific to call.
- **Consistency:** This uses the same public SDK sanitizer composition as the merged Google Chat patch while keeping the change at Telegram's source-of-truth outbound adapter boundary.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** Related open PR scan: no direct linked open PR for this Telegram sanitizer hook. A current open PR search for Telegram sanitizer/tool-trace follow-ups returned broad outbound or unrelated Telegram work, not this focused adapter hook gap.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** The merged anchor #95084 covered Google Chat only; it did not add or test `telegramOutbound.sanitizeText`.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** The source-of-truth owner for Telegram channel delivery behavior is the Telegram outbound adapter, and this patch wires the existing canonical sanitizer hook there instead of adding downstream string filtering or send-path special cases.
- **Local real behavior proof advantage:** The proof imports the production Telegram outbound adapter module and calls its real sanitizer hook directly, showing the exact text transformation that the shared delivery path will apply before send.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram outbound text removes assistant-visible internal tool-trace failure lines before delivery while preserving ordinary assistant text.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.2.2, commit `f85bb708b6`. The command imports the actual Telegram outbound adapter production module through `tsx` and calls its channel sanitizer hook with the required payload shape.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx -e 'import { telegramOutbound } from "./extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts"; const trace = "Done.\n⚠️ 🛠️ `search \"Pipeline\" in /tmp/openclaw-workspace-* (agent)` failed"; console.log(JSON.stringify({ sanitizedTrace: telegramOutbound.sanitizeText?.({ text: trace, payload: { text: trace } }), ordinary: telegramOutbound.sanitizeText?.({ text: "The pipeline has 3 deals.", payload: { text: "The pipeline has 3 deals." } }) }, null, 2));'
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "sanitizedTrace": "Done.",
  "ordinary": "The pipeline has 3 deals."
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production Telegram outbound adapter sanitizer drops the internal tool-trace failure line and returns the expected assistant-visible text. A normal outbound reply remains unchanged.
- **What was not tested:** Outside this proof: native client rendering and Bot API round-trip. Credentials, chat identifiers, and message content are kept out of this PR. The exercised boundary is the adapter sanitizer hook immediately before send.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts`, with dependency guard coverage from `src/infra/outbound/sanitize-text.test.ts` and `src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A Telegram outbound reply containing `Done.` plus an assistant-visible tool-trace failure line sanitizes to `Done.`, while ordinary text like `The pipeline has 3 deals.` is preserved exactly.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression test exercises the Telegram adapter contract that the shared delivery path reads, so it catches the missing hook without requiring network delivery or a mocked Telegram API.

## CI failure attribution

- **Current-head failing check:** `check-test-types` on the initial PR head `20ea432337`.
- **Attribution:** Current PR directly introduced the failure. CI ran `pnpm check:test-types`, which failed only in the two newly added `extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts` sanitizer assertions because the test calls omitted the required `payload` property for `ChannelOutboundAdapter.sanitizeText`.
- **Fix:** The follow-up commit updates both test calls to pass `payload: { text }`.
- **Verification:** The failing extensions test typecheck shard now passes locally:

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo

Passed with no TypeScript errors.
```

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
- extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts passed: 12 tests
```

```text
corepack pnpm format:check -- extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts

All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts extensions/telegram/src/telegram-outbound.test.ts

Completed without lint findings.
```

```text
git diff --check

Completed without whitespace errors.
```

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `message-delivery` and `compatibility`; not `auth-provider`, `session-state`, `security-boundary`, or `availability`.
- **Risk explanation:** This touches a message-delivery adapter hook, but it uses existing SDK sanitizer exports and does not add config, protocol, schema, dependency, target resolution, chunking, or Bot API request behavior.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is two Telegram files, the source change is one adapter property, and focused proof covers both the stripping behavior and preservation of ordinary outbound text.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Telegram's outbound adapter lacked the `sanitizeText` contract hook, so `src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts` could not run the canonical assistant-visible delivery sanitizer for Telegram before normalized payload delivery. That missing source-of-truth mapping caused internal tool-trace failure text to remain in outbound Telegram text.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The shared delivery pipeline already owns when channel sanitizer hooks run; Telegram only needed to expose the correct adapter hook. Adding `sanitizeText` at `extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts` fixes the missing source mapping before send instead of masking the symptom later in Telegram send code.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High — the patch is limited to the Telegram outbound adapter contract and its colocated regression test, with focused production-function proof plus sanitizer dependency tests.
- **Patch quality notes:** No fallback, broad catch, default-return shim, config compatibility branch, or downstream string special case was added. The current PR diff remains only the two Telegram files; the CI follow-up only corrects the test call shape for the existing adapter contract.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** Source-of-truth boundary is the `ChannelOutboundAdapter.sanitizeText` contract as implemented by the Telegram plugin adapter; the shared delivery path already consumes that contract before downstream send/payload handling.

target.title: Fix WebChat dispatch failure session status
target.body: ## Summary - Persist a terminal failed session lifecycle event when WebChat dispatch rejects before an assistant reply. - Preserve terminal lifecycle errors from embedded-agent/provider failures when model fallback is exhausted, so fallback assistant text no longer clears the pending failure before the grace timer finalizes the session. - Broadcast the matching `sessions.changed` payload with `status: "failed"` and accurate `hasActiveRun` so Control UI can clear the active run state. - Add regression tests for pre-agent dispatch rejection, aborted dispatch rejection, embedded lifecycle-error finalization, exhausted fallback handling, and legacy session-key persistence. ## Root Cause The WebChat `chat.send` async dispatch error path cleaned up the active run and broadcast a chat error, but it did not update persisted session lifecycle state. That left `sessions.list` able to return `status: "running"` with `hasActiveRun: false`, which kept the Control UI in a permanent running state. For the real embedded-agent provider rejection path, the embedded run emitted `lifecycle:error`, but a later non-terminal fallback/assistant event cleared the pending terminal lifecycle error before the grace timer finalized it. The session therefore stayed persisted as `running` even though the active run had ended. ## Validation - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.list-changed.test.ts` - `pnpm tsgo:test:src` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/gateway/server-chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts` ## Real behavior proof - Behavior addressed: WebChat sessions no longer remain `status: "running"` with no active run after an embedded AIGW provider 400 before reply. - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR worktree using `atlassian-aigw/openclaw-localtest-missing-model-84352`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: started `openclaw-local-test --repo /Users/jmerhi/repos/claw-working-space/openclaw-pr-84352-review --state-dir ~/.openclaw-local-test-pr84352-provider-400-final --model openclaw-localtest-missing-model-84352 --no-channels --ttl 2h`, sent a `chat.send` WebSocket request to the local Gateway, then waited for the lifecycle grace window. - Evidence after fix: local logs showed `400 "Bad Request"`, `LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.`, and `Embedded agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.` - Observed result after fix: `~/.openclaw-local-test-pr84352-provider-400-final/agents/super/sessions/sessions.json` showed the affected session with `status: "failed"`, non-null `endedAt`, and non-null `runtimeMs`. - What was not tested: Slack/Discord channel delivery; local proof used WebChat/Gateway only with channels disabled. - Video evidence:captures the fixed WebChat run-status behavior locally. This is local evidence and is not committed. https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c69aab1f-fb02-44fb-bcdf-c3355909004e

target.title: feat(xiaomi): add MiMo V2.5 models to the pay-as-you-go provider
target.body:
## Summary

The MiMo V2.5 series (`mimo-v2.5`, `mimo-v2.5-pro`) is Xiaomi's latest model
generation; the V2 series (`mimo-v2-flash`, `mimo-v2-pro`, `mimo-v2-omni`) is
slated for discontinuation and will then route upstream to its V2.5
counterpart. But the `xiaomi` (pay-as-you-go) provider only exposed the V2
catalog — only `xiaomi-token-plan` listed the V2.5 models — so pay-as-you-go
users could not pick the current generation during onboarding, even though
`https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1` already serves it.

This change adds `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `mimo-v2.5` to the `xiaomi` provider
catalog, ordered ahead of the V2 rows so they surface first in the onboarding
model picker. The V2 rows and the V2 default (`xiaomi/mimo-v2-flash`) are kept
for continuity until the upstream discontinuation lands; a follow-up can flip
the default and trim the V2 catalog once Xiaomi's routing is in effect.

## Changes

- `extensions/xiaomi/openclaw.plugin.json`: add `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `mimo-v2.5`
  to the `xiaomi` provider `models` list, before the V2 entries.
  - Model specs match the shared MiMo V2.5 stack: 1,048,576-token context,
    131,072 max output, `reasoning: true`; `mimo-v2.5` accepts `text` + `image`.
  - Pricing uses the published pay-as-you-go (overseas, USD per million tokens)
    rates: `mimo-v2.5` input `$0.14` / output `$0.28` / cache-read `$0.0028`;
    `mimo-v2.5-pro` input `$0.435` / output `$0.87` / cache-read `$0.0036`.
    Pay-as-you-go has no context-length tiered pricing, so no `tieredPricing`
    is set (unlike the Token Plan rows).
  - Also corrects the existing `xiaomi-token-plan` `mimo-v2.5` / `mimo-v2.5-pro`
    `maxTokens` from a stale `32000` to the official `131072` (128K) so the
    identical model ids report the same max output on both providers.
- Reasoning, thinking profiles, and `isModernModelRef` already key off the MiMo
  reasoning model ids across both Xiaomi providers, so no runtime code changes
  were needed — the new rows inherit correct behavior automatically.
- `extensions/xiaomi/onboard.test.ts`: update expected `xiaomi` model ordering
  (default and merge-with-overrides cases) to include the V2.5 rows.
- `extensions/xiaomi/index.test.ts`: assert the full static `xiaomi` catalog
  ordering plus reasoning/input flags for the new V2.5 rows.
- `docs/providers/xiaomi.md`: add the V2.5 rows to the pay-as-you-go catalog
  table and config example, and update the model-details notes to reflect that
  `mimo-v2.5` / `mimo-v2.5-pro` are available on both providers.

## Data sources

- Pricing: https://mimo.mi.com/docs/en-US/price/pay-as-you-go
- Model specs: https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5 and
  https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5-pro
- Max output (131,072 / 128K) confirmed via the live OpenRouter models API
  (`top_provider.max_completion_tokens` for `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` and
  `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro`), context window 1,048,576.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: The `xiaomi` pay-as-you-go provider now exposes
  `mimo-v2.5` and `mimo-v2.5-pro`, so they are selectable during onboarding and
  model selection alongside the existing V2 models.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout built from this branch
  (`pnpm openclaw` builds dist + bundled plugin assets, then runs
  `node scripts/run-node.mjs`), macOS, Node 22+. The `xiaomi` provider uses
  static catalog discovery, so enumerating the catalog needs no API key.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm openclaw models list --provider xiaomi`
- Evidence after fix:

```
$ pnpm openclaw models list --provider xiaomi
Model                                      Input      Ctx         Local Auth  Tags
xiaomi/mimo-v2-flash                       text       256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-omni                        text+image 256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-pro                         text       1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5                           text+image 1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro                       text       1024k       no    no
```

- Observed result after fix: The live `xiaomi` catalog now lists
